column4: Rhetorical theory, English language and linguistics, history of English language, queer theory
column8: I tend to associate "yonder" with the US South and not Victorian speech
column10: Completely plausible as Regency or Victorian text
column12: Dirt as impure/immoral is a HUGE theme in Victorian London. Not that AI couldn't replicate that but it strikes me as something that an expert would know that a machine may not? Idk. The long and winding syntax really seemed Victorian to me though
column16: Style feels Victorian but I'm struck by the first few clauses, which don't seem to make sense. What does "reveal its appearance" mean? Like, I get what it could mean but it seems hardly necessary to say. Likewise, "the walls...appear to have been originally covered by a wall" feels sloppy
column20: Most aristocratic houses would not have "knockers" but night watchmen and/or footmen who open the door for guests! So this felt anachronistic to me. I also think the antithetical construction of "The great ladies do X" while "the less ladies do Y" feels more modern than the more elliptical structure of most Victorian prose. I'm not sure women would be referred to as "lesser" ladies, either. That wouldn't be polite and anyone invited to a ball in the Season would likely have been from an aristocratic background anyway
column24: I struggled with this one a bit more but ultimately veered onto the side of machine-generated because it felt like it wasn't addressing specific concepts or ideas but generalities
column28: This feels like a Victorian man working through his ideas in a somewhat meandering way
column32: This is plausibly human-authored but honestly what makes me think it was machine generated is that it's a touch more concrete and clear than a lot of Victorian prose
column36: My main reason for assuming this was written by a machine is that Gilfil is an unusual name. There is a story about a Mr. Gilfil written by George Eliot, but I didn't remember this quote. Also a parsonage would usually be maintained by the church and not by a lay family, I think.
column38: Completely plausible as Regency or Victorian text
column42: Completely plausible as Regency or Victorian text
column44: Recognize this from Matthew Arnold! First chapter of Culture and Anarchy. Sorry! I think anyone with a PhD in English was probably forced to read this a dozen times
column48: This just feels like bad writing that you might get from someone who's trying to sound sophisticated (which, to be fair, Victorian authors often did). "So sad a hue" feels very Victorian, though. But then I began to wonder if it is an example of machine-generated text precisely because it's mimicking a known cadence.
column50: Completely plausible as Regency or Victorian text
column52: Famous quote! I think it's missing a bit though
column56: Not much to say here--just gut instinct. This is the one I really don't have  a good reason to feel either way
column60: The London Season was more of a late spring/Summer thing, not something in February
column63: Some Regency/Victorian elements but mostly modern
column64: This may be human generated, but I don't think it was actually written by a Victorian. The first sentence is a fragment--not something Victorians tended to do. The second sentence doesn't make sense: the working classes were not paid a lot by any means. Often aristocrats were able to staff massive staffs precisely because wages were so low
column66: Completely plausible as Regency or Victorian text
column68: Couldn't really tell with this one, but I am pretty sure that the L&MR Railway was the first purely locomotive one, so...maybe a human did write this? I suppose a machine could find that info too but I'm going with gut
column72: I wasn't sure what St. John would have to do with the observation of the sabbath. Like, I get that he had some part in creating the idea of the sabbath but how does his reference fit into the paragraph as a whole?
column75: Some Regency/Victorian elements but mostly modern
column79: Some Regency/Victorian elements but mostly modern
column80: This doesn't read like other nature guides I've read from the period. It sounds like someone directly addressing an audience ("I now proceed to give you") as opposed to the more empirical/imperial/authoritative tone nature writers of the era did
column84: Very plausibe but the the following part tripped me up and made me think it as a machine trying to sound sensible rather than a human trying to make a point: " for if it were possible so long as all other kinds had been made on the same principle, there would be no occasion to increase its cost by any additional outlay, but rather to reduce the price per ton from "

column8: "solemn murmur" and "yonder" feel less modern than a parody of Victorian style
column12: "heavy lazy mist" feels modern, though the rest sounds Victorian, esp the syntax.
column14: Completely plausible as Regency or Victorian text
column16: The specificity of the proper names and the somewhat archaic punctuation (e.g., --:) make it seem genuinely Victorian.
column20: This is a tough one. It's accurate as far as social convention goes, but the grammar and syntax as well as the short sentences scream ChatGPT. God help me if I'm wrong.
column24: Boring as hell so most likely authentic Victorian.
column28: "reduced to practice"? This is an odd phrase, possibly machine made.
column32: Cumbersome and repetitious. The machine's trying too hard.
column36: Are a lot of these passages taken from Trollope novels? He's big on parsonages.
column40: Heavy-handed alliteration and the phrase "brick-and-mortar" came of age in the 1990s Amazon period.
column44: Plume, smattering and badge are all nice figurative language -- and Victorian plausible.
column47: Some Regency/Victorian elements but mostly modern
column48: Bad undergraduate writing ("decaying fragments", the wind sweeping up, etc)?
column50: Completely plausible as Regency or Victorian text
column56: Several awkward repetitions that make it seem like the machine is trying too hard to generate a calcified style. Then again, the Victorians often wrote a calcified style.
column60: Several different registers mixed up. A chairman and members don't belong with a Great House open for the season.
column64: Sounds like bad Macaulay in his review of Southey's Colloquies. The first sentence is a fragment. I've never read a sentence fragment by a Victorian writer.
column66: Completely plausible as Regency or Victorian text
column68: Historically and stylistically accurate. Good fake if it is one.
column71: Some Regency/Victorian elements but mostly modern
column72: Crappy prose. The first sentence is a comma splice. Victorians generally knew their grammar.
column76: I think it's human, though not entirely sure.
column80: It's going well until "more details than has been before done", which sounds awkward in a way a machine trying to be Victorian might sound awkward.
column84: The currency denominations toward the end give away this passage.
